title: Pediatric peritoneal dialysis: exchanges and care
executiveSummary: Perform pediatric peritoneal dialysis exchanges using an aseptic technique, with volumes adjusted to body surface area, caregiver education, and monitoring for peritonitis in accordance with pediatric ISPD guidelines.
body:
## Steps to perform

1. Verify patient identification, current weight, and prescription (volume per exchange, dwell time, concentration, CAPD/APD modality).
2. Involve a trained caregiver; explain the steps to the child in an age-appropriate manner.
3. Perform hand hygiene; prepare single-use sterile supplies.
4. Reduce air drafts; wear a mask if required by institutional protocol.
5. Disinfect the catheter cap and connections (using circular friction for ≥ 15 seconds); allow to dry.
6. **Drainage:** connect the tubing set; drain completely; record the volume and appearance of the effluent.
7. **Infusion:** connect the dialysate bag, purge any air; infuse the prescribed volume based on weight/body surface area.
8. Close clamps; disconnect using an aseptic technique; place a sterile cap on the catheter.
9. Discard used supplies; record the time, volumes, concentration, and any incidents.
10. Weigh the child; monitor for abdominal pain, fever, nausea, or cloudy effluent.
11. In case of cloudy effluent: do not re-infuse; notify immediately; draw an effluent sample for culture and cell count.

## Scientific rationale

### Pediatric volumes

| Parameter | Guidance |
| --- | --- |
| Fill volume | 800–1100 ml/m² of body surface area (adjust to tolerance) |
| Initial volume in infants | Lower volume; progressive increments according to nephrology guidance |
| Dwell time | According to prescription; frequently longer dwell times in infants |

Recalculate volumes with every significant change in weight or height.

### Evidence and recommendations

> **ISPD Pediatric Guidelines (2020):** Pediatric PD preserves growth and quality of life; an aseptic technique and caregiver education are essential (Schaefer et al., 2020).

> **Pediatric peritonitis:** Presentation may be atypical in infants (irritability, low-grade fever); cloudy effluent is the cardinal sign (Li et al., 2016).

### Caregiver education

- Step-by-step connection/disconnection technique.
- Proper dialysate storage and maintaining a clean environment.
- Warning signs: cloudiness, fever, pain, fluid leakage.
- 24-hour contact availability with the pediatric nephrology team.

### Pediatric monitoring

- Weight and height measurement at every follow-up check (growth percentiles).
- Blood pressure monitoring adjusted for age.
- Nutritional status and serum protein levels.
- School attendance and adapted physical activity.

### Complications — notify immediately

- Peritonitis (cloudy effluent ± fever/pain).
- Dialysate leak or hernia.
- Catheter obstruction.
- Dehydration or fluid overload.
- Exit-site infection.

bibliography.citation: Schaefer F, et al. ISPD Guidelines for Peritoneal Dialysis in Children. Perit Dial Int. 2020.
Li PK, et al. ISPD Peritonitis Recommendations 2016. Perit Dial Int. 2016.
Warady MA, et al. IPNA clinical practice recommendations for children with CKD. Pediatr Nephrol. 2019.
Brown EA, et al. ISPD Guidelines for Peritoneal Access. Perit Dial Int. 2020.
International Society for Peritoneal Dialysis — pediatric guidelines.
